coredata.dc:title: Two strategies for rapid and sensitive detection of procalcitonin using carbon dots-encapsulated nanocapsule and magnetic carbon dots as coupled labels

Lu, Siyu
Yang, Kun
Dou, Xuechen
Feng, Xiangling
Cui, Haoran
Li, Fan
Tian, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Procalcitonin (PCT) is a crucial biomarker for diagnosing bacterial infections in clinical settings. Here, a biosensing system utilizing a fluorescence amplification system of nanocapsules and magnetic carbon dots was developed. The system incorporated two PCT sensing strategies: highly sensitive immunomagnetic separation and rapid homogeneous immunoassay. In these strategies, nanocapsules modified with antibodies function as immunosensors and energy transmitters, while magnetic carbon dots modified with antibodies serve as capture probes and immunosensors. The immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy utilized immunonanocapsules as sensors and immunomagnetic carbon dots as capture probes. This strategy demonstrated the capability to achieve ultrasensitive trace detection of PCT within the range of 1–1000 pg/mL, following normal immunoreactivity and magnetic separation. The limit of detection to 0.3 pg/mL (serum) and 0.33 pg/mL (plasma). Via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer principle, the homogeneous immunoassay strategy used immunonanocapsules as energy transmitters and immunomagnetic carbon dots as sensors. This enabled direct and rapid detection of PCT in a wide range of 0–100 ng/mL in diluted serum, with a limit of detection of 0.41 ng/mL. Both strategies demonstrated high recoveries (93.5%-104.6% and 98.4%-103.8%) and exhibited reliability in achieving accurate quantitative PCT detection. Such a novel analytical approach to PCT may broaden the potential uses of carbon dots-based immunofluorescent sensing. Furthermore, it was direct implications for ultrasensitive and rapid point-of-care detection of various serum biomarkers in clinical settings.

head.ce:title: Two strategies for rapid and sensitive detection of procalcitonin using carbon dots-encapsulated nanocapsule and magnetic carbon dots as coupled labels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Systems Engineering Institute, Academy of Military Sciences, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Systems Engineering Institute, Academy of Military Sciences, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Military Medical Sciences, Academy of Military Sciences, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Military Medical Sciences, Academy of Military Sciences, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Serial B-CDs@BONs and R-CDs@MNPs were prepared and optimized as multifunctional sensors for detecting procalcitonin (PCT). The two detection strategies, the rapid homogeneous immunoassay strategy and the ultrasensitive magnetic separation analysis strategy could be flexibly and selectively employed based on B-CDs@BONs and R-CDs@MNPs. Via the FRET principle, the homogeneous immunoassay strategy realized rapid quantitative analysis of PCT (0-100 ng/mL) in diluted serum with a LOD of 0.41 ng/mL. The immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy enabled sensitive PCT (1–1000 pg/mL) analysis in serum and plasma under an external magnetic field with LODs of 0.3 pg/mL and 0.33 pg/mL, respectively. Moreover, the two strategies showed high recovery rates (93.5%–104.6% and 98.4%–103.8%).

Procalcitonin (PCT) is a crucial biomarker for diagnosing bacterial infections in clinical settings. Here, a biosensing system utilizing a fluorescence amplification system of nanocapsules and magnetic carbon dots was developed. The system incorporated two PCT sensing strategies: highly sensitive immunomagnetic separation and rapid homogeneous immunoassay. In these strategies, nanocapsules modified with antibodies function as immunosensors and energy transmitters, while magnetic carbon dots modified with antibodies serve as capture probes and immunosensors. The immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy utilized immunonanocapsules as sensors and immunomagnetic carbon dots as capture probes. This strategy demonstrated the capability to achieve ultrasensitive trace detection of PCT within the range of 1–1000 pg/mL, following normal immunoreactivity and magnetic separation. The limit of detection to 0.3 pg/mL (serum) and 0.33 pg/mL (plasma). Via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer principle, the homogeneous immunoassay strategy used immunonanocapsules as energy transmitters and immunomagnetic carbon dots as sensors. This enabled direct and rapid detection of PCT in a wide range of 0–100 ng/mL in diluted serum, with a limit of detection of 0.41 ng/mL. Both strategies demonstrated high recoveries (93.5%-104.6% and 98.4%-103.8%) and exhibited reliability in achieving accurate quantitative PCT detection. Such a novel analytical approach to PCT may broaden the potential uses of carbon dots-based immunofluorescent sensing. Furthermore, it was direct implications for ultrasensitive and rapid point-of-care detection of various serum biomarkers in clinical settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Procalcitonin (PCT) is a widely used biomarker for diagnosing and treating sepsis [1–6]. The concentration of PCT may indicate the severity of the disease [7–9], which is utilized to establish clinical guidelines for sepsis stratification and monitoring of antimicrobial treatment [1,3,10–12]. Various methods are available for detecting PCT, such as microfluidic immunoassay, fluorescence immunoassay, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, colloidal gold colorimetry, and electrochemiluminescence immunoassay [2,8,9,13,14]. Notably, the fluorescent immunosensing assay method based on specific antigen–antibody interaction possesses short analysis time and excellent specificity characteristics [15]. However, its high complexity and complex instrument requirements may limit its practical application [2,13,16]. Therefore, there is a need to develop a rapid, efficient, convenient-to-operate, and highly-sensitive fluorescent immunosensor for PCT detection, which poses a significant challenge that deserves attention.

Carbon dots (CDs) are fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with low chemical inertness, tunable luminescence, affordability, and excellent biocompatibility [17–24]. Due to their exceptional optical properties and highly sensitive sensing capability, CDs are widely used in biosensing applications [17,21–23,25,26], such as the detection of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) [27,28]. Notably, there have been limited studies on the detection of PCT using CDs. Previously, we employed the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle and utilized fluorescence-matched CDs to develop a rapid homogeneous immunoassay for PCT in 500-fold diluted serum [29]. However, the method had limitations regarding anti-background interference and its detection sensitivity, which hindered the application of carbon dot-based immunoprobes in biosensing. Previous research has demonstrated that high fluorescence intensity markers exhibited more resistance to background signal interference and superior detection accuracy [30,31]. Therefore, effectively amplifying fluorescence signals is crucial for improving detection sensitivity and reducing the limit of detection (LOD).

Nanocapsules have been widely pertained in drug/gene delivery, biological imaging, catalysis, and biosensing [32–34]. The preparation of nanocapsules is easily attained via mild reaction conditions, ensuring the efficient encapsulation of nanoparticles and preventing leakage [35–37]. Additionally, it revealed fluorescent nanoparticles' enrichment ability and amplifying fluorescent signal function [34,37,38]. Currently, to the best of our knowledge, CDs encapsulated nanocapsules have not been used in rapid and ultra-sensitive detection of PCT. Immunomagnetic carbon dots have gained significant attention in analytical chemistry and biosensing [39–43] due to their specific capture and recognition capabilities in complex samples, eliminating the need for complex preprocessing procedures [39,40]. Inspired by the above idea, we will propose a novel biological system based on fluorescence-amplified nanocapsules and immunomagnetic separation of magnetic carbon dots for quantitative PCT detection. Utilizing an immunomagnetic separation analysis and homogeneous immunoassay strategy, this interactive biological system enables rapid, efficient, and highly sensitive detection of PCT.

In this study, strong blue fluorescent emitting carbon dots-encapsulated nanocapsules and red fluorescent carbon dots coated-magnetic carbon dots were used as immunolabels. Normally, the concentration of PCT in the bloodstream is typically below 0.25 ng/mL [1,8]. However, an increase in PCT concentration can indicate sepsis’s severity. PCT levels, i.e., 0.25–0.5 ng/mL, 2 ng/mL, and 10 ng/mL, are associated with mild or localized bacterial infection, systemic bacterial infection (sepsis), and sepsis or septic shock, respectively [2,4,8,12,13,44]. Based on the above indicators, The immunomagnetic separation analysis and homogeneous immunoassay strategies successfully detected PCT in pg and ng levels, respectively. Both strategies showed high recoveries, ranging from 93.5% to 104.6% and 98.4% to 103.8%, respectively. The novel biosensing system provides a new approach to the clinical analysis of PCT. It can potentially broaden the application of carbon dot-based immunofluorescent detection to other serum biomarkers in clinical settings, enabling ultrasensitive and rapid point-of-care detection.

The citric acid (AR), Ethylenediamine (AR), Triton X-100 (AR), n-hexanol (AR), Cyclohexane (AR), 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (AR), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tetraethyl orthosilicate (AR) was provided by Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ammonia solution (30%) was supplied by Chemical Reagent Wholesale Company (Tianjin, China). (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (AR) and Bis (Triethoxysilylpropyl) Disulfide (AR) were acquired by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Carboxylated magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were received from Biotyscience Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). PCT monoclonal antibodies and PCT were purchased from Bioss Biotech Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Rabbit plasma was obtained from the Animal Center of Tianjin Medical Research Institute, and rabbit serum was obtained from rabbit plasma. Deionized (DI) water (18.0 MΩ·cm) was produced in our laboratory.

B-CDs and R-CDs were prepared in a vacuum drying oven (LC-213, Shanghai Espec Environmental Equipment Co., Ltd.). The TEM and element mappings images were taken on a 300 kV Talos F200S G2 (Thermo Fisher, USA) electron microscope. The SEM images were taken on Tescan Mira LMS, Czech. The FT-IR spectra were measured on a Thermo FTIR spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FS5, UK). The XRD spectra were performed at Rikuta Smartlab SE (Japan). The XPS analysis was measured on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA). The Zeta potential of samples was taken on Zeta potential analyzer (Nano ZS, Malvern Panalytical, UK). The UV–vis spectra were determined by the Tianmei UV2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai). The VSM analysis was measured on LakeShore7404 (USA).

Blue emission carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method described in a previous study [29]. 2.1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 60 mL DI water. Then, 2.68 mL of ethylenediamine was added to the solution and carbonized at 200 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, the obtained product was cooled to room temperature and dialyzed (in a 500 Da dialysis bag) for 48 h. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

Some brief modifications were made to prepare nanocapsules (BONs) as in previous reports [37]. 1.7 mL of Triton X-100 and 1.8 mL of n-hexane were dissolved in 7.5 mL of cyclohexane to form solution A. 45, 91, 228, and 465 μL of B-CDs (25 mg/mL) were combined with 0.04 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate and 0.09 mL of bis (3-(triethoxymethylsilyl) propyl) disulfide to form solution B, respectively. Solutions A and B were then blended, and 0.05 mL of 30% ammonia solution was added. The mixture was stirred at 26 °C for 20 h. 0.05 mL of 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane was added to the above mixture. After 12 h of reaction, 20 mL of acetone was added to precipitate xB-CDs@BONs (x = 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1), respectively. The above xB-CDs@BONs were washed and centrifuged with ethanol and DI water for several cycles. Finally, the serial product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

Red emission carbon dots (R-CDs) were prepared using the hydrothermal method described in a previous study [45]. 0.18 g of 2,5 diaminobenzene sulfonic acid was dissolved in 60 mL DI water and carbonized at 200 °C for 10 h. The obtained product was cooled to room temperature and dialyzed (in a 500 Da dialysis bag) for 24 h. Finally, the red-brown powder was obtained by freeze-drying and stored in a sealed container at 4 °C.

Four batches of MNPs (1 mL, 10 mg/mL) were taken and mixed with 40 mg of EDC. The mixture was vigorously shaken and incubated in a shaker for 30 min. Then, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mg of R-CDs were orderly added to each MNPs suspension batch. Next, 20 mg of NHS was added to the mixture and left overnight in a shaker. An external magnetic field was applied to remove unreacted substances, and the suspension was washed several times with PBS. Finally, the resulting xR-CDs@MNPs suspensions (x = 2, 3, 4, 5) were re-suspended and stored at 4 °C.

The immunofluorescent nanocapsules were prepared as follows: Firstly, 0.2B-CDs@BONs was passed through a 0.8 μm pinhole filter, and the filtrate was collected. Next, 120 mg of EDC and 30 mg of NHS were added to 1 mL of 0.2B-CDs@BONs (1, 2, 5, 10 mg/mL). After vigorous shaking, 10 μL of Monoclonal antibody-1 (1 mg/mL) was added to the above dispersion and stayed overnight at 4 °C. The mixture was centrifuged and washed several cycles with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C.

The preparation of immunomagnetic carbon dots followed a similar procedure to that of immunofluorescent nanocapsules. 120 mg of EDC and 30 mg of NHS were added to 1 mL 4R-CDs@MNPs (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 mg/mL). The mixture was vigorously shaken, and then 10 μL of Monoclonal antibody-2 (1 mg/mL) was added to the dispersion. The mixture was left overnight under a shaker. Subsequently, the mixture was separated using an external magnetic field, underwent several cycles of washing with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4), and finally stored at 4 °C.

The optimization of the immunofluorescent nanocapsules involved the following steps: 30 μL of 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1 (1, 2, 5, 10 mg/mL) were taken on 1 mL of PCT solution (0.7 ng/mL), 30 μL 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 were added to the above solution, incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. The products were collected by an external magnetic field and redispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured at 360 nm excitation conditions.

The immunomagnetic carbon dots' optimization was as follows, 10 μL of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 mg/mL) were taken on 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured at 360 nm excitation conditions. The best fluorescence emission sample in 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 (0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 mg/mL) was identified by this method. 10, 20, 30, and 40 μL of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 were taken on 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4), respectively. The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured at different excitation conditions (360, 440, 480 nm). The effect of probe concentration on fluorescence could be determined by this method.

The immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy in PBS was conducted as follows. 30 µL of immunofluorescent nanocapsules and 30 µL of immunomagnetic carbon dots were blended with 1 mL of PCT (1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000 pg/mL), respectively. After 20 min of incubation (37 °C), the mixture was collected by an external magnetic field and redispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured under 360 nm excitation conditions.

The fluorescence amplification effect of the nanocapsules was investigated in this section. 30 μL of immunofluorescent nanocapsules and B-CDs were taken in 1 mL PCT solution (200 pg/mL). Next, 30 μL of immunomagnetic carbon dots were added to the above solution. After 20 min of incubation (37 °C), the mixture was collected by an external magnetic field and redispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). Finally, the fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured under 360 nm excitation conditions.

The homogeneous immunoassay strategy in PBS was conducted as follows. 30 µL of immunofluorescent nanocapsules and 30 µL of immunomagnetic carbon dots were blended with 1 mL of PCT (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 ng/mL), respectively. After 20 min of incubation (37 °C), the fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured under 360 nm excitation conditions.

Rabbit serum was obtained from the plasma of experimental rabbit hearts using centrifuge processing. PCT's magnetic separation detection strategy in serum was similar to that in PBS. Rabbit serum was used in the incubation medium instead of 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). Finally, after 20 min of incubation (37 °C), the mixture was collected by an external magnetic field and redispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured under 360 nm excitation conditions.

The fluorescence spectra of rabbit serum (diluted 0, 10, 50, 100 times) were measured in 360 nm excitation condition. Then, 30 µL of immunomagnetic carbon dots were added to the above rabbit serum, and the fluorescence spectra of the solution were recorded at 360 nm excitation condition. The fluorescence shielding effect of different concentrations of rabbit serum on immunomagnetic carbon dots could be identified by this method. Finally, PCT's homogeneous immunoassay strategy in serum was similar to that in PBS. Rabbit serum was used in the incubation medium instead of 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4).

Rabbit plasma was obtained from the experimental rabbit heart plasma. PCT's immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy in plasma was similar to that in PBS. Instead of 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4), rabbit plasma was used in the incubation medium. Finally, after 20 min of incubation (37 °C), the mixture was collected by an external magnetic field and redispersed in 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). The fluorescence spectra of the solution were measured at 360 nm excitation conditions.

The fluorescence spectra of rabbit plasma (diluted 0, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1000 times) were measured in 360 nm excitation condition. Then, 30 µL of immunomagnetic carbon dots were added to the above rabbit plasma, and the fluorescence spectra of the solution were recorded at 360 nm excitation condition. The fluorescence shielding effect of different concentrations of rabbit plasma on immunomagnetic carbon dots could be identified by this method. Finally, the homogeneous immunoassay strategy of PCT in plasma was similar to that in PBS. Instead of 1 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4), rabbit plasma was used in the incubation medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and sensing response of immunoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 illustrates the synthetic procedures for the serial xB-CDs@BONs and xR-CDs@MNPs samples and the two strategies for detecting PCT based on the immunofluorescent nanocapsules and immunomagnetic carbon dots. Fig. 1A and 1B briefly describe the preparation routes for serial xB-CDs@BONs and xR-CDs@MNPs, respectively. Fig. 1C demonstrates the preparation of immunofluorescent nanocapsules and immunomagnetic carbon dots and their two detection strategies toward PCT. In PCT's homogeneous immunoassay strategy, 0.2B-CDs@BONs was excited at 360 nm excitation condition. The FRET pathway between 0.2B-CDs@BONs and 4R-CDs@MNPs was established by PCT, resulting in the fluorescence emission (639 nm) of 4R-CDs@MNPs, indicating PCT concentration dependence. In the immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy, PCT was explicitly captured by immunomagnetic carbon dots and sensitively sensed by immunofluorescent nanocapsules. The immunofluorescent nanocapsules' fluorescence emission (ca. 450 nm) demonstrated a concentration-dependent enhancement of PCT at 360 nm excitation condition. However, the two effects were not observed in the absence of PCT. Therefore, via the predetermined standard curves of PCT, the two rapid and ultra-sensitive detection strategies of serial PCT solutions could be achieved within 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structures of serial xB-CDs@BONs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and SEM analyses were implemented to observe the morphology and structures of xB-CDs@BONs. The morphology of xB-CDs@BONs displayed a uniform spherical structure (Fig. S1). xB-CDs@BONs revealed preeminently dispersibility in aqueous solution (Fig. S1C, S1F, S1I, S1L). B-CDs were stably encapsulated within xB-CDs@BONs, and there were noticeable variations in the number of encapsulated B-CDs in serial xB-CDs@BONs (Fig. 2 A-2D). Additionally, the lattice spacing (0.17 nm) of B-CDs could be observed under HRTEM, consistent with our previous report [29]. The elemental mappings of serial xB-CDs@BONs revealed the presence of C, N, O, and Si elements, confirming the stable encapsulation of B-CDs within xB-CDs@BONs (Fig. S2). Moreover, there were no broken after cycled ultrasonic and centrifugal treatment, which proved that B-CDs were stably encapsulated in xB-CDs@BONs. These results could preliminarily demonstrate that the nanocapsules of serial xB-CDs@BONs had been acquired successfully. Additionally, the particle size distribution of serial xB-CDs@BONs was relatively uniform (Fig. S3). The average particle sizes for 0.1B-CDs@BONs, 0.2B-CDs@BONs, 0.5B-CDs@BONs, and 1B-CDs@BONs were measured to be 80.2 ± 20.8 nm, 77.8 ± 15.7 nm, 82.9 ± 15.9 nm, and 83.7 ± 14.7 nm, respectively (Fig. 2E). Moreover, the different contents of B-CDs had no obvious effect on the particle size of xB-CDs@BONs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of physical and chemical evaluation of xB-CDs@BONs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, encapsulated efficiency, FT-IR, XPS, XRD, zeta potential, and fluorescence performance of xB-CDs@BONs were performed. Different ratios of B-CDs were encapsulated in silicone shells to form xB-CDs@BONs. Fig. 3 A shows the preparation process of xB-CDs@BONs. Briefly, via hydrolysis, silicone shells were prepared by Tetraethyl orthosilicate and Bis(triethoxysilylpropyl) disulfide. B-CDs were ideally encapsulated into the interior to form nanocapsules with B-CDs as the core and organosilica as the outer shell.

The additional analysis of B-CDs using fluorescence spectroscopy revealed concentration differences in their fluorescence performance. Fig. S4 showed the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs within the concentration range of 0.01–1 mg/mL. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs exhibited an initial increase followed by a decrease, with the peak value observed at a concentration of 0.15 mg/mL. Furthermore, the position of the fluorescence emission peak was found to be correlated with the concentration, exhibiting a red shift from ca. 443 nm (0.01 mg/mL) to 467 nm (1 mg/mL). The fluorescence emission intensity showed an excellent linear correlation with the concentration of B-CDs in four-stage segments, i.e., 0.01–0.1, 0.1–0.15, 0.15–0.2, and 0.2–1 mg/mL (R2 > 0.97). At concentrations below 0.15 mg/ml, the core structure of B-CDs remained unaffected by its surroundings. Consequently, the fluorescence emission of individual B-CDs dominated the overall fluorescence spectrum [46,47]. The linear increase in fluorescence intensity further suggested that the increase in fluorescence emission was solely due to the increase in B-CDs. The red shift observed in the fluorescence emission peak was typically a result of the strong coupling of surface functional groups, potentially leading to a substantial increase in the non-irradiated transition rate [46].

B-CDs' encapsulation efficiency in nanocapsules could be easily measured by the property of B-CDs. Fig. 3B depicted the encapsulation efficiency of B-CDs in serial xB-CDs@BONs, which were over 88%. B-CDs' encapsulation number varied in serial xB-CDs@BONs, as confirmed by TEM. Additionally, Fig. 3C shows the XPS spectra, which indicate that xB-CDs@BONs are composed of C, N, O, and Si elements. Five peaks appear, i.e., 102.1, 153.1, 285.1, 400.1, and 532.1 eV on the entire XPS spectra, corresponding to the binding energies of Si2p, Si2s, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, proving the surface of xB-CDs@BONs indeed included Si, C, N, and O, which also were confirmed by EDS. Furthermore, Fig. 3D displayed the peaks at 101.3, 101.8, and 102.5 eV in the Si2p spectrum, representing Si-C, Si-N, and Si-O groups, respectively [48]. Fig. 3E revealed the peaks at 284.5, 285.8, 286.4, and 288.5 eV in the C1s spectrum, representing C-C/C-Si, C-N, C-O, and C=O groups, respectively [49].

The functional groups on the surface of B-CDs and xB-CDs@BONs were characterized using FT-IR, as shown in Fig. 3F. The absorption peak at 1083 cm−1 in the xB-CDs@BONs spectrum was attributed to the vibration absorption peak of Si-O-Si [37,48]. The absorption peaks at 1640, and 3430 cm−1 could be attributed to amino groups' stretching and bending vibrations, respectively [48,49], indicating the presence of amino groups on the surface of xB-CDs@BONs. In Fig. 3G, it can be observed that B-CDs had a negative zeta potential of −6.6 mV. In contrast, the serial xB-CDs@BONs exhibited significantly higher negative zeta potentials of −36.5, −35.2, −34.6, and −33.9 mV, respectively. Moreover, the zeta potential values showed a concentration-dependent trend, confirming the successful preparation of xB-CDs@BONs. Via XRD analysis, it was found that broad diffraction peaks in the range of 15-30° were detected, consistent with pristine B-CDs (Fig. S5A). Additionally, As shown in Fig. S5B, xB-CDs@BONs had a characteristic absorption peak at 350 nm, which might be attributed to the surface group's n-π* (C=O) transition with a lone pair of electrons [48].

Fig. S5C showed that the primary excitation wavelength of xB-CDs@BONs was ca. 360 nm. In Fig. 3H, the fluorescence spectra of xB-CDs@BONs were displayed. It is worth noting that the fluorescence emission of xB-CDs@BONs varies depending on the excitation wavelength (Ex.). When the Ex. increased, the emission peak shifted towards longer wavelengths. This behavior is likely associated with the distribution of surface states in xB-CDs@BONs. Furthermore, the property of excitation-dependent PL emission made it easier to create FRET systems [49]. The emission wavelength of the transmitter (xB-CDs@BONs) could be easily controlled, facilitating energy matching between the donor and acceptor. Fig. 3I shows the fluorescence emission spectra of serial xB-CDs@BONs (Ex. 360 nm). It was observed that 0.2B-CDs@BONs exhibited superior fluorescence performance compared to the other samples, and there was a significant difference in fluorescence peak intensity compared with other samples (Fig. S5D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structures of serial xR-CDs@MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM analyses were implemented to observe the morphology and structures of R-CDs, MNPs, and xR-CDs@MNPs. The as-prepared R-CDs were well scattered with particle sizes of 2.5 ± 1.5 nm (Fig. S6) and a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (Fig. 4 A). The particle size of the MNPs was relatively uniform, with a particle size of 10 ± 1.5 nm (Fig. S6) and a lattice spacing of 0.31 nm (Fig. 4B). The elemental mappings of the MNPs primarily revealed the presence of Fe and O elements, with a slight content of the C element (Fig. S7A). The TEM of xR-CDs@MNPs displayed that those ratios of R-CDs had no apparent effect on the morphology of xR-CDs@MNPs (Fig. 4C-4F). Two types of spherical crystals in HRTEM of xR-CDs@MNPs were observed with the measured lattice spacings of 0.25 nm and 0.31 nm, corresponding to R-CDs and MNPs, respectively. Both R-CDs and MNPs coexisted in the serial xR-CDs@MNPs. The elemental mappings of serial xR-CDs@MNPs contained all elements of R-CDs and MNPs, and the content of C and S elements gradually increased in serial xR-CDs@MNPs (Fig. S7B-S7E). Furthermore, the absence of separation between R-CDs and MNPs after undergoing cycled ultrasonic and magnetically separable treatment indicated a relatively compact combination. These findings provide preliminary evidence of the successful acquisition of the serial xR-CDs@MNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of physical and chemical evaluation of xR-CDs@MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, overlap efficiency, FT-IR, XRD, zeta potential, and fluorescence performance of xR-CDs@MNPs were performed. The concentration dependence of R-CDs' fluorescence intensity in the range of 0.02–1 mg/mL was observed, and the peak position red-shifted with the concentration change (Fig. S8B and S8C). R-CDs were connected to MNPs through amide bonds, which enhanced fluorescence intensity and improved stability (Fig. S8D) due to the surface plasmon resonance coupling between Fe3O4 and R-CDs [50]. Therefore, the fluorescence spectroscopy method was unsuitable for calibrating R-CDs' loading efficiency. The color of R-CDs' solution showed concentration dependence of PCT in the range of 0.02–5 mg/mL (Fig. S8E and S8F). Moreover, it was found that RGB value (R/B) vs. R-CDs concentration was well fitted as linearization (Fig. S8E). And the linear equation was y = 1.01x + 1.04. Via this method, R-CDs' loading efficiency exceeds 60% in all the serial xR-CDs@MNPs (Fig. 5 A).

In addition, the magnetic properties of MNPs and serial xR-CDs@MNPs were investigated using VSM (Fig. 5B). The magnetic profiles of both are depicted in Fig. 5B. Both MNPs and xR-CDs@MNPs showed strong magnetism. The saturation magnetization of xR-CDs@MNPs was R-CDs' concentration-dependent, with values of 62.5, 44.5, 44.0, 42.0, and 39.2 emu/g, respectively. MNPs could be collected within 20 s by an external magnetic field when dispersed in PBS (Fig. S9). Therefore, in future experiments, the serial xR-CDs@MNPs nanocomposites can be easily separated and collected using an external magnetic field.

Via XRD analysis (Fig. 5C), it was found that the diffraction peaks of xR-CDs@MNPs were detected corresponding to the crystal planes (220), (311), (511), and (400) of Fe3O4 [51,52]. In addition, a broad diffraction peak appeared within 15–20° consistent with pristine R-CDs (inset in Fig. 5C), which could indicate the successful magnetic functionalization of R-CDs. Fig. 5D shows the FT-IR spectra of R-CDs, MNPs, and serial xR-CDs@MNPs. The absorption peak of serial xR-CDs@MNPs in 547 cm−1 was caused by the vibration absorption peak of Fe-O-Fe [51], which was consistent with MNPs. The two absorption peaks in 2687 and 2949 cm−1 might be caused by amino stretching vibration. However, MNPs did not exhibit a similar peak, providing evidence that the amino groups on the surface of xR-CDs@MNPs were introduced by R-CDs. The two absorption peaks in 1554 and 1641 cm−1 proved the formation of amide bonds, confirming that MNPs and R-CDs in xR-CDs@MNPs were tightly combined through amide bonds.

Fig. S10A showed that the primary excitation wavelength of xR-CDs@MNPs was ca. 480 nm. Whereas the external excitation wavelength and 0.2B-CDs@BONs' fluorescence emission wavelength, the fluorescence emission of serial xR-CDs@MNPs under excitation conditions of 360, 440, and 480 nm was measured. Fig. 5E shows the fluorescence emission spectra of serial xR-CDs@MNPs (Ex. 440 nm). It was observed that 4R-CDs@MNPs exhibited superior fluorescence performance compared to other samples. There was a significant difference in the fluorescence peak intensity at 639 nm compared to other samples (Fig. S10B), further confirmed by the excitation conditions of 360 and 480 nm (Fig. S10C-S10F). It was hypothesized that under the given ratio condition, 4R-CDs@MNPs exposed more R-CDs sites than other samples. Fig. 5F showed that R-CDs had a negative zeta potential of −1.9 mV, while pure MNPs exhibited a significantly higher negative zeta potential of −27.6 mV. Interestingly, the zeta potential of xR-CDs@MNPs ranged from −20.9 to −16.2 mV, indicating that it fell between the values observed for R-CDs and pure MNPs. This suggests that the formation of xR-CDs@MNPs influenced the surface charges of pure MNPs, providing further evidence for the successful preparation of xR-CDs@MNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of physical and chemical evaluation of immunofluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: To precisely capture and recognize PCT, nanocapsules and magnetic carbon dots were modified by monoclonal antibody pairs, and immunofluorescent nanocapsules and immunomagnetic carbon dots were prepared. TEM analysis confirmed that PCT monoclonal antibodies could form complexes with 0.2B-CDs@BONs and 4R-CDs@MNPs, as depicted in Fig. 6 A and 6D. The distribution of 0.2B-CDs@BONs on the surface of antibody1 (Ab1) appeared uniform, while 4R-CDs@MNPs were densely packed around antibody2 (Ab2).

To further validate monoclonal antibody pairs modification of nanocapsules and magnetic carbon dots, FT-IR, FL, UV–vis, and zeta potential of immunofluorescent probes were performed. Fig. 6C shows the FT-IR spectra of 0.2B-CDs@BONs, Ab1, and 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1. The absorption peak in 1100 cm−1 was caused by the antisymmetric stretching peak of Si-O-Si, which was consistent with 0.2B-CDs@BONs. The absorption peak in 2721 cm−1 may be associated with amino stretching vibration. The two firm absorption peaks in 1562 and 1648 cm−1 proved the formation of amide bonds [29,53], confirming the successful preparation of immunofluorescent nanocapsules. Fig. 6E indicates the FT-IR spectra of 4R-CDs@MNPs, Ab2, and 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2. The absorption peak in 547 cm−1 was caused by the vibration absorption peak of Fe-O-Fe, which was consistent with 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2. The two absorption peaks in 2705 and 2971 cm−1 might be caused by amino stretching vibration. The two distinct absorption peaks in 1562 and 1648 cm−1 proved the formation of amide bonds [29,53], confirming the successful preparation of immunofluorescent magnetic carbon dots.

In addition, the fluorescence properties of immunofluorescent probes were evaluated. The fluorescence emission peaks of 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1 were detected, corresponding to the two characteristic peaks (410 and 445 nm) of Ab1 (Fig. 6C). Moreover, a broad emission peak around 450 nm, consistent with the pristine 0.2B-CDs@BONs, was observed. The fluorescence intensity at this peak was significantly higher compared to Ab1. Furthermore, the characteristic emission peak of 4R-CDs@MNPs was also detected around 639 nm (as shown in Fig. 6F). These findings were further supported by the UV–vis spectra of immunofluorescent nanocapsules and immunomagnetic carbon dots (Fig. S11A and S11B). Next, the excitation spectrum of 4R-CDs@MNPs sufficiently overlapped with the emission spectrum of 0.2B-CDs@BONs (Fig. S11C), which indicated that 0.2B-CDs@BONs and 4R-CDs@MNPs could be used as energy donor–acceptor pair in FRET sensors [54]. Fig. S11D showed that Ab1 had a negative zeta potential of −6.8 mV, while pure 0.2B-CDs@BONs had a high negative zeta potential of −35.8 mV. However, the zeta potential 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1 was −15.7 mV, between Ab1 and 0.2B-CDs@BONs, respectively. The same trend also occurred in Ab2, 4R-CDs@MNPs, and 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2. The complexation of antibodies with 0.2B-CDs@BONs and 4R-CDs@MNPs may have influenced the surface charges of these materials, providing evidence for the successful preparation of immunofluorescent probes.

A series of ratios of nanocapsules and monoclonal antibodies were explored to prepare immunofluorescent nanocapsules with excellent fluorescence properties. The fluorescence emission spectra of immunofluorescent nanocapsules were analyzed in Fig. 7 A at the same concentration, under excitation at 360 nm. The peak fluorescence intensity was observed at a ratio of 500. Fig. 7B compares the intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks of the immunofluorescent nanocapsules. The intensity of the fluorescence emission peak was significantly different in samples with a ratio of 100 and 200 compared to the sample with a ratio of 500. However, there was no significant difference in the intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks of the sample with a ratio of 1000. It was suggested that the number of nanocapsules on the surface of Ab1 had reached saturation when the ratio was 500. To compare the fluorescence amplification effect of immunofluorescent nanocapsules with immunofluorescent B-CDs, PCT with a 200 pg/mL concentration was detected under the same conditions. The result in Fig. 7C showed that the fluorescence emission peaks of the immunofluorescent nanocapsules were 2.83 times more intense than those of the immunofluorescent B-CDs (Fig. 7D), which confirmed the superior sensing ability of immunofluorescent nanocapsules.

A series of ratios of magnetic carbon dots and monoclonal antibodies were explored to prepare immunomagnetic carbon dots with excellent fluorescence properties. The fluorescence emission spectra of the immunomagnetic carbon dots were analyzed in Fig. 7E under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm at the same concentration. The peak fluorescence intensity was observed when the ratio was 50. In Fig. 7F, a comparison of the intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks of the probes was shown. The intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks was significantly different in samples with a ratio of 10 and 20 compared to the sample with a ratio of 50. It was suggested that the number of magnetic carbon dots on the surface of Ab2 had reached saturation when the ratio was 50. However, the intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks was also significantly different in the sample with a ratio of 100 compared to the sample with a ratio of 50. This difference in intensity may be attributed to the background interference of 4R-CDs@MNPs when the ratio was 100, resulting in a slightly lower transmittance of the solution. Therefore, based on the above result, we investigated the fluorescence emission of immunomagnetic carbon dots at different concentrations. Fig. 7G displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of these probes under 360 nm excitation. The results indicated that the fluorescence emission intensity of the probes reached its maximum peak at 30 μg/mL and then decreased (Fig. 7H). It was guessed that high concentrations of 4R-CDs@MNPs might interfere with the fluorescence sensing of the probe, producing background interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy for PCT
ce:section.ce:para: For the preliminary evaluation of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 and 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1 detection ability towards PCT with different concentrations, the fluorescent spectra were first determined in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). It was presupposed that PCT was explicitly captured by immunomagnetic carbon dots and sensitively sensed by immunofluorescent nanocapsules. The fluorescence emission of the immunofluorescent nanocapsules (450 nm) exhibited a concentration-dependent enhancement of PCT under 360 nm excitation. The fluorescence intensity of 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1 showed concentration dependence of PCT (1–1000 pg/mL) (Fig. 8 A). Moreover, it was found that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) vs. PCT concentration was well fitted as linearization (Fig. 8B). And the linear equation was y = 0.019x + 2.04 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), with a LOD of 0.25 pg/mL (by 3δ/slope). However, there was no linear within F/F0 vs. PCT concentration (greater than 1000 pg/mL) (Fig. S12). Such phenomena confirmed the hypothesis and prompted us to explore the situation for detecting PCT in the actual sample (Fig. 8C–8F).

As shown in Fig. 8C and 8E, the fluorescence intensity of 0.2B-CDs@BONs-Ab1 still showed concentration dependence of PCT (1–1000 pg/mL). Fig. 8D and 8F showed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) had a good linear correlation with the concentration of PCT in serum and plasma, respectively. the linear equations were y = 0.018x + 2.44 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99) and y = 0.018x + 2.29 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), respectively. The LOD was 0.3 pg/mL and 0.33 pg/mL, which were lower than in previous studies (Table S1). The serum and plasma samples analysis showed that the detailed linear fitting results were excellent and comparable to PBS, indicating that the strategy could specifically identify and capture PCT while minimizing background interference. The recoveries in PBS, serum, and plasma were 99.2–103.4%, 93.5–104.6%, and 98.4–103.8%, respectively (Table S2). These relatively high recovery rates demonstrate the reliability of the strategy and its ability to accurately quantify PCT. Surprisingly, with 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 as a sensing probe, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 also had a good linear correlation with the concentration of PCT (1–1000 pg/mL), further supporting the authenticity of the strategy (Fig. S13). Three batches of immunosensor demonstrated excellent repeatability for PCT (100 pg/mL) detection, with a standard error of 1.3% (Fig. S14A). The performance of the immunosensor stored at 4 °C for 7 days showed a reduction of 2.3% compared to the fresh immunosensor, indicating good stability of the immunosensor (Fig. S14B). Furthermore, the prepared immunosensor exhibited high specificity for PCT detection (Fig. S14C). Therefore, the strategy could be used for the ultra-sensitive and specific detection of PCT, providing a valuable foundation for the clinical detection of PCT.

Previous studies have demonstrated that the 0–100 ng/mL quantitative detection of PCT could be achieved between B-CDs-Ab1 and R-CDs-Ab2 via the principle of FRET. It was presupposed that 0.2B-CDs@BONs would be excited at 360 nm excitation conditions. It was assumed that 0.2B-CDs@BONs would be excited under 360 nm excitation conditions. A FRET pathway was established between 0.2B-CDs@BONs and 4R-CDs@MNPs through PCT, resulting in fluorescence emission at approximately 639 nm from 4R-CDs@MNPs. This emission indicated a dependence on the concentration of PCT. To evaluate the detection ability of the immunoprobes for different concentrations of PCT, the fluorescent spectra were initially measured in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). The fluorescence intensity 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 exhibited a concentration dependence on PCT in the 0–100 ng/mL (Fig. 9 A). Moreover, it was found that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) vs. PCT concentration was well fitted as linearization (Fig. 9B). And the linear equation was y = 0.024x + 1 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), with a LOD of 0.34 ng/mL. This phenomenon confirmed our hypothesis and motivated us to investigate the detection of PCT in actual samples (Fig. 9C-9F).

The high background interference of serum and plasma might be due to cholesterol, glucose, protein, and substances [55,56]. Firstly, the fluorescence spectra of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 in different serum dilutions were studied. It was observed that there was no impact on the fluorescence emission of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 when the serum dilution factor exceeded 50 times (Fig. S15). In 50-fold diluted serum, the fluorescence intensity of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2 showed concentration dependence of PCT (0–100 ng/mL) (Fig. 9C), consistent with the trend in PBS. Moreover, it was found that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) vs. PCT concentration was well fitted as linearization (Fig. 9D). And the linear equation was y = 0.01x + 1 (n = 3, R2 = 0.99), with a LOD of 0.41 ng/mL. Compared to our previous study, this work had a lower LOD. The probes had more vital shielded antiserum ability and had more diverse functions (Fig. S16). Three batches of immunosensor demonstrated prominent repeatability for PCT (100 ng/mL) detection, with a standard error of 2.3% (Fig. S17A). The performance of the immunosensor stored at 4 °C for 7 days showed a reduction of 1.8% compared to the fresh immunosensor, indicating good stability of the immunosensor (Fig. S17B). Furthermore, the prepared immunosensor exhibited high specificity for PCT detection (Fig. S17C). However, the strategy described in Fig. 9E could not be applied to the natural plasma. It was observed that even a 1000-fold dilution of plasma completely shielded the fluorescence emission of 4R-CDs@MNPs-Ab2, as confirmed by Fig. S18. The analysis ability of the strategy was evaluated in the pristine and 1000-fold diluted plasma. The fluorescence spectra did not visibly change when different concentrations of PCT (20, 40, 60, 80, 100 ng/mL) were added, compared to when no PCT was added (Fig. S19). Via counting the fluorescence intensity value at 639 nm, it was found that there was no significant difference (Fig. 9F). The recoveries in PBS and 50-fold diluted serum were 99.7–103.8% and 98.4–101.5%, respectively (Table S3). The relatively high recovery rate indicates that the strategy is reliable, with excellent detection accuracy for quantitative PCT. Additionally, the wide detection range and simple operation process enable quick evaluation of the infection degree in patients.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of two sensing strategies for PCT
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of PCT in actual samples can be achieved with high sensitivity and a wide detection range using immunofluorescent nanocapsules and immunomagnetic carbon dots. However, there are notable differences between the two strategies. Firstly, the sensing strategy differs between the two approaches. The immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy utilizes the specific capture ability of immunomagnetic carbon dots and the high fluorescence sensing performance of immunofluorescent nanocapsules. However, this strategy involves a more complex operation process due to the requirement of an external magnetic field for screening. The homogeneous immunoassay strategy simplifies the operation process by eliminating separation and washing steps via the FRET mechanism between immunofluorescent nanocapsules and immunomagnetic carbon dots. Notably, immunofluorescent nanocapsules exhibit enhanced anti-background signal interference ability, making them more suitable for analyzing complex samples and offering better sensitivity. On the other hand, the homogeneous immunoassay strategy demonstrates low sensitivity due to high background signal interference, rendering it unsuitable for more complex actual samples. In conclusion, while the two strategies may differ, they are mutually beneficial, and their integration could offer a unique approach to clinical PCT analysis (See Fig. 10 ).

In this work, the as-prepared 0.2B-CDs@BONs represented more excellent fluorescence performance in serial xB-CDs@BONs, and B-CDs were ideally encapsulated inside the capsule. Additionally, serial magnetic carbon dots was easily achieved, with 4R-CDs@MNPs demonstrating excellent comprehensive performance. The biosensing system, which utilized antibody-modified 0.2B-CDs@BONs and 4R-CDs@MNPs, exhibited exceptional specificity and excellent analysis ability for PCT in the two strategies of immunomagnetic separation analysis and homogeneous immunoassay, respectively. Via the sensing system, rapid and ultrasensitive quantitative detection of PCT could be easily realized in actual samples. The immunomagnetic separation analysis strategy applied to ultrasensitive trace analysis of PCT at the pg level (1–1000 pg/mL) and the LOD in serum and plasma were 0.3 pg/mL and 0.33 pg/mL, respectively, which had the anti-background solid ability and high sensitivity. Additionally, a homogeneous immunoassay strategy was employed to analyze PCT rapidly at the ng level. The LOD was determined to be 0.41 ng/mL (in 50-fold diluted serum), making it easily operable and offering a wide detection range of 0–100 ng/mL. In summary, the magnetic and optical interaction strategies effectively meet the requirements for clinical PCT detection. This novel biosensing system holds promise as a new method for the clinical analysis of PCT. It introduces new possibilities for ultrasensitive and rapid point-of-care detection of various biomarkers in clinical settings.
